did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed EnsembleSkinNet, an ensemble deep learning framework using transfer learning with pre-trained CNN architectures (Modified VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3, and DenseNet201) for skin cancer classification. It utilized the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets to achieve diagnostic results, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and employed Grad-CAM for explainable AI integration.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' in the header. It presents the design and implementation of a new framework (EnsembleSkinNet), describes specific methods (transfer learning, softmax-weighted spectrum fusion, Grad-CAM), and reports original experimental results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) on the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets.

title: EnsembleSkinNet: a transfer learning-based framework for efficient skin cancer detection with explainable AI integration
brief_summary: This study presents EnsembleSkinNet, an ensemble deep learning framework for skin cancer classification that integrates transfer learning with Modified VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3, and DenseNet201 architectures. The model utilizes softmax-weighted spectrum fusion and Bayesian hyperparameter optimization to enhance robustness and generalization across diverse imaging conditions. Evaluation on the HAM10000 dataset via five-fold cross-validation yielded an accuracy of 98.32%, precision of 98.20%, recall of 98.10%, and an F1-score of 98.15%. Cross-domain generalization was validated on the ISIC 2020 dataset, achieving an external validation accuracy of 96.84% and an AUC of 0.983. Additionally, Grad-CAM-based explainability analysis demonstrated a mean Explainability Accuracy of 93.6%, indicating strong agreement with dermatologist annotations. The results suggest that EnsembleSkinNet offers a robust, interpretable, and clinically viable tool for early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Seven skin lesion classes (Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into seven diagnostic categories using the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets. The model predicts discrete lesion classes via softmax-weighted spectrum fusion of CNN architectures. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,015 dermatoscopic images of skin lesions, categorized into seven classes. It was used for model training, validation, and testing via five-fold cross-validation. The study notes that the dataset primarily consists of images from individuals with fair skin.

use.training: Used for training and validation via five-fold cross-validation (70% training, 15% validation, 15% testing splits per fold). Data was split at patient/lesion level to prevent leakage. Duplicate filtering (SSIM > 0.95) and data augmentation were applied to the training set.
use.validation: Used for validation during the five-fold cross-validation process (15% of data per fold) and for hyperparameter tuning via Bayesian optimization.
use.testing: Used for testing during the five-fold cross-validation process (15% of data per fold) to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC 2020 Challenge containing 2,357 dermoscopic images obtained with different imaging protocols. It was used for external validation to assess cross-domain generalization.
classes_used: Not explicitly listed in the context, but implied to be skin lesion classes compatible with the HAM10000 taxonomy.

use.training: None. The model was tested on this dataset without any re-training or fine-tuning.
use.testing: Used for external validation/testing to evaluate domain generalization capability. The model achieved 96.84% accuracy and 0.983 AUC on this dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance and compute mean metrics with standard deviation across different data partitions.
To calculate 95% confidence intervals for primary metrics (Accuracy, F1-score) to estimate variability without assuming normality.
To evaluate the null hypothesis that all models perform equally across multiple datasets or folds (non-parametric significance testing).
To conduct pairwise comparisons between models to identify significant differences in performance after rejecting the null hypothesis in the Friedman test.
To measure agreement between Grad-CAM explainability analysis and dermatologist annotations.

hypothesis_testing.test: Friedman test followed by Wilcoxon signed-rank post-hoc tests
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: All models have equivalent median ranks across folds.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: At least one model differs significantly in performance from the others (specifically, EnsembleSkinNet outperforms baseline models).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Friedman test yielded a p-value of 0.0006, rejecting the null hypothesis and indicating statistically significant performance differences among the compared models. Post-hoc Wilcoxon signed-rank tests confirmed that EnsembleSkinNet significantly outperformed each baseline model (p < 0.05) across all evaluated metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score).

HAM10000.experiment: Five-fold cross-validation (mean ± SD over 5 folds and 5 random seeds)
HAM10000.notes: Statistical significance was assessed using Friedman test (p=0.0006) and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests (p < 0.05 for EnsembleSkinNet vs baselines). 95% bootstrap confidence intervals were computed for primary metrics.

ISIC 2020.notes: EnsembleSkinNet outperformed single models by 2-3% in accuracy. The best single baseline (DenseNet201) achieved an accuracy of 95.42 ± 0.51% and AUC of 0.971 in some referenced comparisons, but Table 6 reports 94.75% accuracy for DenseNet201 on this specific external validation set.

Explainability.notes: Indicates strong agreement with dermatologist annotations. False negatives for melanoma and basal cell carcinoma were noted to be measurable less.

architecture_used.main_model: EnsembleSkinNet (Ensemble of Modified VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3, and DenseNet201)
architecture_used.pretraining: All four backbone models were initialized with weights pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in the provided context.

M-VGG16_modifications: Batch Normalization after each convolutional block
Replacement of fully connected layers with Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer
architecture_details.ensemble_fusion_strategy: Softmax-weighted spectrum fusion (weighted average of predictions normalized via softmax function based on validation accuracy)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Initial layers frozen; last convolutional block retained for task-specific learning
architecture_details.classifier_head: Global Average Pooling (GAP) transforming feature map to vector, followed by a fully connected dense layer and dropout mechanism, then softmax activation for class probabilities

training_details.epochs: Up to 50 epochs with early stopping on validation loss
training_details.hyperparameter_tuning: Bayesian Optimization for dropout rate, number of trainable layers, learning rate, and batch size

classification_or_output_head.output: Class probabilities for seven lesion types
classification_or_output_head.fusion: Weighted average of softmax probabilities from the four models

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: 18.9M (for M-VGG16 backbone only; total ensemble parameter count not explicitly summed)

preprocessing_techniques: Duplicate filtering using Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) and pixel-level histogram correlation to remove near-duplicate pairs (SSIM > 0.95)
Normalization of pixel values to the range [0, 1]
Patient- and lesion-level stratified splitting to prevent data leakage
Data augmentation on the training set only: random rotations (±25°), horizontal and vertical flipping, zooming (scale 0.8–1.2), and brightness/contrast adjustment (±10%)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotations (±25°), horizontal and vertical flipping, and zooming (scale 0.8–1.2) applied to the training set to improve class balance and generalization.
Brightness/contrast adjustment (±10%) applied to the training set to create a near-balanced training distribution (~2000 images/class).
Applied before augmentation to mitigate copy influence; used Structural Similarity Index (SSIM > 0.95) and pixel-level histogram correlation to identify and remove near-duplicate pairs.

conclusions: EnsembleSkinNet, an ensemble deep learning framework using softmax-weighted spectrum fusion of Modified VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3, and DenseNet201, achieved superior performance compared to individual baseline models and other state-of-the-art methods.
On the HAM10000 dataset, EnsembleSkinNet achieved an accuracy of 98.32 ± 0.41%, precision of 98.20 ± 0.35%, recall of 98.10 ± 0.38%, and F1-score of 98.15 ± 0.37% via five-fold cross-validation.
The model demonstrated strong cross-domain generalization on the external ISIC 2020 dataset, achieving an accuracy of 96.84 ± 0.42% and an AUC of 0.983 without re-training.

Grad-CAM-based explainability analysis yielded a mean Explainability Accuracy of 93.6% (Cohen's Kappa = 0.87), indicating strong agreement with dermatologist annotations and confirming that the model focuses on clinically significant regions.

Statistical analysis using the Friedman test (p = 0.0006) and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests confirmed that the performance improvements of EnsembleSkinNet over baseline models were statistically significant (p < 0.05).
The framework effectively reduced false negatives for melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, thereby improving early detection rates and diagnostic confidence.

Ablation studies confirmed that architectural diversity contributes to robustness, with DenseNet201 and ResNet50 providing the most substantial individual contributions, while the full 4-model ensemble outperformed smaller ensembles (2 or 3 models).
Softmax-weighted averaging was identified as the most effective fusion strategy, yielding the highest accuracy and lowest variance compared to stacking meta-learners and logit-level blending.

The study acknowledges that the HAM10000 dataset primarily consists of images from individuals with fair skin, which may limit generalization to darker skin tones, though the model showed robustness across diverse imaging conditions in the ISIC 2020 validation.
important_remarks: The HAM10000 dataset primarily consists of images from individuals with fair skin, creating a demographic bias that limits generalization to darker skin tones and diverse ethnicities.

The study lacks testing on actual clinical data or multi-center trials; validation was limited to public datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC 2020), restricting evidence of real-world clinical deployment robustness.

The model was trained on HAM10000 and tested on ISIC 2020 without re-training or fine-tuning, which assesses cross-domain generalization but does not reflect scenarios where models are adapted to specific clinical environments.

The explainability analysis (Grad-CAM) relies on a mean Explainability Accuracy of 93.6% against dermatologist annotations, but the paper acknowledges this lacks standardized quantitative evaluation methods for explainability.

The framework requires significant computational resources due to the ensemble of four large CNN architectures (Modified VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet201), which may hinder deployment on edge devices or resource-constrained settings.

Performance on minority classes (e.g., Dermatofibroma with 115 samples, Vascular Lesions with 142 samples) may be less reliable due to the inherent class imbalance in the training data, despite augmentation efforts.

Pre-training weights were derived from ImageNet (natural images), which may not fully reflect dermatological feature distributions, potentially limiting feature specialization compared to domain-adapted pre-training.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset for model development, including training, validation, and internal testing via a five-fold cross-validation protocol. The model was pre-trained on ImageNet. For external validation, the trained model was tested on the ISIC 2020 dataset without any re-training or fine-tuning to assess cross-domain generalization.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset and tested on the ISIC 2020 dataset. This constitutes a cross-dataset validation experiment as the test data (ISIC 2020) is a different named cohort from the training data (HAM10000), and the model was not re-trained or fine-tuned on the test set.

training.counts: 70% of the dataset per fold (approx. 7,010 images total, balanced to ~2000/class via augmentation)
training.notes: Used for model fitting. Data was split at patient/lesion level. Duplicate filtering (SSIM > 0.95) was applied. Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zoom, brightness/contrast) was applied only to the training set.

validation.counts: 15% of the dataset per fold (approx. 1,502 images total)
validation.notes: Used for hyperparameter tuning (Bayesian optimization) and model selection (determining ensemble weights via softmax-weighted spectrum fusion based on validation accuracy).

testing.counts: 15% of the dataset per fold (approx. 1,502 images total)
testing.notes: Used for internal performance evaluation within the five-fold cross-validation scheme.

external_testing.notes: Used for external validation to assess domain generalization. The model was tested without re-training or fine-tuning.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Pre-trained weights for the constituent CNN architectures (Modified VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3, DenseNet201) were derived from ImageNet. The first layers were kept static to preserve generic feature approximations.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation techniques (random rotations ±25°, horizontal/vertical flips, zoom 0.8-1.2, brightness/contrast ±10%) were applied to the training set to create a near-balanced distribution (~2000 images/class) and improve generalization.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: Five-fold stratified cross-validation on HAM10000. Each fold consisted of 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% testing subsets. The split was performed at the patient/lesion level to prevent data leakage. Results were averaged over 5 folds and 5 random seeds.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 5-fold cross-validation. Folds stayed within the HAM10000 dataset. Patient/lesion-level splitting ensured no overlap between training, validation, and test sets within each fold.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: The ISIC 2020 dataset (2,357 images) was used as unseen external data for cross-domain generalization assessment. The model was not trained on this dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results are mean ± SD over 5 folds and 5 random seeds. Evaluated on the test partition of each fold.
External validation on unseen data. No re-training or fine-tuning was performed. Outperformed single baselines (DenseNet201, ResNet50) by 2-3% in accuracy.
Best performing single baseline model on HAM10000.
Best performing single baseline model on ISIC 2020 external validation.

cross_dataset_experiments.notes: The model trained on HAM10000 was applied directly to ISIC 2020 without any adaptation. This demonstrates cross-domain generalization capability.

within_dataset_results.split_or_cv: 5-fold cross-validation (70% train, 15% val, 15% test per fold)

generalizability_notes: The HAM10000 dataset primarily consists of images from individuals with fair skin, which may limit generalization to darker skin tones.
The study lacks testing on actual clinical data or multi-center trials; validation was limited to public datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC 2020).
The model was trained on HAM10000 and tested on ISIC 2020 without re-training, assessing cross-domain generalization but not adaptation to specific clinical environments.

Performance on minority classes (e.g., Dermatofibroma with 115 samples, Vascular Lesions with 142 samples) in the training data may be less reliable due to class imbalance, despite augmentation efforts.
Pre-training weights were derived from ImageNet (natural images), which may not fully reflect dermatological feature distributions.
information_not_reported: Specific counts of images in the ISIC 2020 dataset per class.
Detailed breakdown of the 5 random seeds used for the external validation results (only mean ± SD is reported for ISIC 2020).
Specific patient demographics for the ISIC 2020 dataset beyond 'different imaging protocols'.
